details.question: What makes a good test case?
details.reference_answer: A good test case should be clear, detailed, and simple. It must contain basic components like Test Case ID, Title, Description, Preconditions, Test Data, Test Steps, Expected Results, Actual Results, Status (Pass/Fail), and Priority. It should cover positive, negative, and boundary scenarios while remaining reusable and independent of other test cases.

metadata.section_title: What Makes a Good Unit Test?
metadata.page_title: Unit Testing in Software Testing
Unit Testing in Software Testing

details.context_text:
[S1] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/unit-testing/ | section=What Makes a Good Unit Test? | score=0.5500
Well, I’m not the right person to tell what makes a good Unit Test, but based on my observations on various projects I can tell the characteristics of a good Unit Test. Bad Unit Tests do not add value to the project. Instead, project costs increase significantly by writing and managing bad Unit Tests. How to write good Unit Tests? Unit tests should be written to verify a single unit of code and not the integration. Small and isolated Unit tests with clear naming would make it very easy to write and maintain. Changing to another part of the software should not affect the Unit test if those are isolated and written for a specific unit of code. It should run quickly. Unit test should be reusable.

[S2] | source=explore_it_reduce_risk_and_increase_confidence_with_exploratory.pdf | score=0.4974
. 2.3 Good Charters A good charter offers direction without overspecifying test actions. As an example, the following isn’t a charter; it’s a test case. When charters are too specific, they become just a different (and weird) way of expressing individual tests. We end up spending a lot of time on test docu- mentation with very little benefit. report erratum • discuss Good Charters • 15

[S3] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/unit-testing/ | section=Best Practice | score=0.4491
To create the best code during this testing, consider the following points: Code should be strong: There are instances where the test fails or in worst cases does not get executed at all if the code is broken. Understandable and reasonable: The code should be easy to understand. This makes it easy for the developer to write the code and even other developers who will work on the code subsequently will find it easy to debug. Should be a single case: Tests that define multiple cases in one are complex to work with. Thus, writing a single case code is a best practice, which makes the code easier to understand and debug. Allow automated tests: The developers should make sure that the test runs in an automated form. It should be in a continuous delivery or integration process

[S4] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/test-case-template-examples/ | section=Conclusion | score=0.4488
Personally, I prefer to use a Test Case Management tool . You can start with an open-source tool. It will be a good addition to your efforts to set up the testing process, and meanwhile, it will also save a lot of time instead of manually maintaining these documents. We have also seen test case templates and a few examples using very good quality documentation. I hope this article was helpful to you. Research Process : Total Time taken for research and publishing this guide : 40 Hours (approx). This guide is based on real testing practices used by QA professionals. We reviewed commonly used test case templates, examined how teams document test cases in day-to-day projects, and created examples that reflect practical testing scenarios

[S5] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/bug-tracking-test-metrics-and-test-sign-off-free-qa-training-day-6/ | section=Test Metrics | score=0.4262
We have established that during the Test Execution phase, reports are sent out to all the other project team members to give a clear idea about what is happening in the QA Execution phase . This information is important to everyone in order to get validation about the overall quality of the final product. Imagine I report that 10 test cases passed or 100 test cases were executed- these numbers are merely raw data and do not give a very good perspective about how things are going on. Metrics play a vital role in filling this gap. Metrics are in simple words, intelligent numbers that the testing team collects and maintains . For Example , if I said 90% of the test cases passed, it makes more sense than saying 150 test cases passed
details.generated_answer:
A good test case is characterized by several key attributes:

1. **Clarity and Understandability**: A test case should be easy to understand, which facilitates both writing and debugging by developers. This clarity ensures that subsequent developers can easily work with the code [S3].

2. **Single Focus**: A good test case should define a single case rather than multiple cases. This simplicity makes the test easier to manage and debug, as complex tests can lead to confusion and difficulty in execution [S3].

3. **Automation Compatibility**: Test cases should be designed to run in an automated manner, allowing them to be integrated into continuous delivery or integration processes. This ensures efficiency and consistency in testing [S3].

4. **Reusability**: Test cases should be reusable, which contributes to maintaining a clean and efficient testing process. This aspect helps in reducing redundancy and improving the overall quality of the testing efforts [S1].

5. **Isolation**: Good test cases should be isolated, meaning that changes in other parts of the software should not affect them. This isolation allows for more reliable testing outcomes [S1].

These characteristics collectively contribute to the effectiveness and efficiency of the testing process, ensuring that the test cases provide valuable insights into the quality of the software being tested.

details.question: How do I write test cases from requirements or an SRS document?
details.reference_answer: Writing test cases from an SRS involves understanding the requirements, identifying functional areas, and mapping requirements to test scenarios. For each scenario, define execution steps, input data, and explicit expected results derived from the SRS. This ensures that test cases map back to requirement specifications and maintain business logic coverage.

details.generated_answer:
To write test cases from requirements or an SRS (Software Requirements Specification) document, you start by thoroughly reviewing the SRS to understand the test objectives. This review involves analyzing the requirements to identify what needs to be tested. The SRS review is a critical step where the QA team, along with development, design, environment, and data teams, collaborates to ensure clarity and completeness of requirements. The outcome of this review helps in creating a Technical Design Document (TDD) and forms the basis for test scenario identification [S1].

After the SRS review, you prepare test scenarios that cover the identified test objectives. These scenarios are derived directly from the requirements specified in the SRS. Once test scenarios are ready, you proceed to write detailed test cases, which are step-by-step instructions to verify each requirement or scenario. Test cases should be clear, concise, and cover both positive and negative conditions to ensure comprehensive testing [S2].

The process typically follows these steps:
1. Walkthrough the SRS document to understand the application and its requirements.
2. Review the SRS to identify test objectives and create test scenarios.
3. Develop a test plan outlining the testing strategy.
4. Write detailed test cases based on the test scenarios, which can be documented using test management tools or spreadsheets for better organization and coverage [S2].

This approach ensures that test cases are directly traceable to requirements, improving test coverage and effectiveness [S1][S2].

details.question: What should a test plan include?
details.reference_answer: A standard test plan includes scope, objectives, features to be tested and not tested, test approach, entry criteria, exit criteria, suspension criteria, environmental requirements, schedule, roles and responsibilities, deliverables, risks, mitigation plans, and approvals.

a) Test Plan Approach/Objective This document includes and describes the information necessary to effectively define the strategy to successfully carry out the testing of the Application Under Test. This also includes the scope of the project testing. It basically gives an idea of what will be covered in the test plan. Example: This Test Plan is documented to describe the predefined scope, approach, resources (hardware & personnel), schedule of all the testing activities of the project STH Ecommerce, way of communication, and the risks associated with and corresponding mitigation plan.

Let’s take a detailed look at the Entry/Exit criteria for System Test. Entry Criteria: The system should have passed the exit criteria of Integration testing i.e. all the test cases should have been executed and there should be no critical or Priority P1, a P2 bug in an open state. Test Plan for this testing should be approved & signed off. Test cases/scenarios should be ready to be executed. Test scripts should be ready to be executed. All the non–functional requirements should be available and test cases for the same should have been created. The testing environment should be ready. Exit Criteria: All the test cases should be executed. No critical or priority or security-related bugs should be in an open state

. Project scope and test focus are defined in the test plan. Basically, it deals with test coverage, features to be tested, features not to be tested, estimation, scheduling and resource management. Whereas the test strategy defines guidelines for test approach to be followed in order to achieve the test objectives and execution of test types defined in the testing plan. It deals with test objectives, approaches, test environments, automation strategies and tools, and risk analysis with a contingency plan. details.generated_answer:
A test plan should include several key components to effectively define the strategy for testing an application. According to the retrieved context, the essential elements of a test plan are as follows:

1. **Introduction**: This section outlines the purpose of the test plan and provides an overview of the testing objectives and scope of the project.

2. **Scope**: It defines what will be covered in the testing process, including the features to be tested and those that will not be tested.

3. **Test Strategy (Approach)**: This section describes the overall approach to testing, including the types of testing to be performed and the methodologies to be used.

4. **Environmental Needs**: It specifies the test environment requirements, including hardware and software configurations necessary for testing.

5. **Staffing and Training Needs**: This outlines the personnel required for testing and any training they may need.

6. **Test Schedule and Estimation**: It includes a timeline for testing activities and estimates of the resources needed.

7. **Test Deliverables**: This section lists the expected outputs from the testing process, such as test cases, test scripts, and reports.

8. **Exit Criteria**: It defines the conditions that must be met for testing to be considered complete, such as the execution of all test cases and the resolution of critical bugs.

9. **Suspension and Resumption Criteria**: This outlines the conditions under which testing may be paused and the process for resuming it.

10. **Responsibilities**: It details the roles and responsibilities of team members involved in the testing process.

11. **Risk and Contingencies**: This section identifies potential risks associated with the testing process and outlines mitigation strategies.

12. **Assumptions**: It lists any assumptions made during the planning process that could impact testing.

13. **Test References**: This includes any relevant documents or standards that inform the testing process.

14. **Review and Approvals**: It specifies the process for reviewing the test plan and obtaining necessary approvals [S1][S2][S5].

details.question: What is the difference between a test plan and a test strategy?
details.reference_answer: A test strategy is a high-level document that defines the overall testing approach, standards, tools, and methods. A test plan is a more detailed and project-specific document that defines scope, schedule, resources, responsibilities, and execution details for testing a particular release or project.

details.generated_answer:
A test plan and a test strategy serve different purposes in the software testing process.

1. **Test Plan**: The test plan defines the project scope and test focus. It outlines what will be covered in testing, including test coverage, features to be tested, features not to be tested, estimation, scheduling, and resource management. Essentially, it provides a vision of what you want to achieve in the testing process [S5].

2. **Test Strategy**: In contrast, the test strategy outlines the guidelines for the test approach to be followed to achieve the test objectives. It includes details on test objectives, approaches, test environments, automation strategies and tools, and risk analysis with a contingency plan. The test strategy acts as an action plan designed to achieve the vision set forth in the test plan [S5].

In summary, the test plan is focused on the "what" and "when" of testing, while the test strategy addresses the "how" of achieving the testing goals.

details.question: What is boundary value analysis and equivalence partitioning?
details.reference_answer: Equivalence partitioning divides input data into valid and invalid classes where the system is expected to behave similarly, and tests representative values from each class. Boundary value analysis focuses on values at the edges of these partitions, such as minimum, maximum, just below minimum, and just above maximum, because defects often occur near boundaries.

details.context_text:
[S1] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/test-case-template-examples/ | section=How to Write Test Cases (Step-by-Step) | score=0.5500
. Step 2: Define Test Data Input data quality determines test case success. Identify the type of inputs needed to run your test cases. Valid Input Data: Regular inputs that will normally work (for example, a regular 10 digit telephone number input). Invalid Input Data: Inputs intended to “crash” the system (for example, entering symbols for a field designated for a name input). Boundary Input Data: Testing the boundaries of an input (for example, testing exactly 8 digits of password if it ranges from 8 to 16 digits). Step 3: Write Clear Steps Execution steps need to be unambiguous enough that even a first-time user without prior experience using the software is able to understand them

[S2] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/tips-to-design-test-data-before-executing-your-test-cases/ | section=How to Prepare Data That Will Ensure Maximum Test Coverage | score=0.5282
Design your data by considering the following categories: 1) No data: Run your test cases on blank or default data. See if proper error messages are generated. 2) Valid data set: Create it to check if the application is functioning as per requirements and valid input data is properly saved in database or files. 3) Invalid data set: Prepare invalid data set to check application behavior for negative values and alphanumeric string inputs. 4) Illegal data format: Make a data set of illegal data formats. The system should not accept data in an invalid or illegal format. Also, check if proper error messages are generated. 5) Boundary Condition dataset: Dataset containing out of range data

details.generated_answer:
Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) and Equivalence Partitioning (EP) are two important test case design techniques used in software testing.

**Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** focuses on testing the boundaries between partitions. It involves identifying the values at the edges of input ranges, as these are often where errors occur. For example, if a field accepts values from 1 to 10, BVA would test the values 1 and 10, as well as values just outside this range, such as 0 and 11. This technique is effective in detecting bugs that occur at the limits of input ranges [S3][S4].

**Equivalence Partitioning (EP)**, on the other hand, divides input data into valid and invalid partitions. The idea is that if one test case from a partition passes or fails, all other cases in that partition will behave similarly. For instance, if a system accepts valid ages from 18 to 65, testing with a value like 30 (valid) and a value like 17 (invalid) would be sufficient to represent the entire partition of valid and invalid ages [S3][S4].

Both techniques are crucial for designing effective test cases that maximize test coverage and help ensure that the application behaves as expected under various input conditions [S2].

details.question: What is use case testing?
details.reference_answer: Use case testing is a black-box test design technique where test scenarios are derived from user interactions with the system. It validates functional requirements from the perspective of an actor and covers main flows, alternative flows, and exception flows.

details.context_text:
[S1] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/unit-testing/ | section=What is Unit Testing? | score=0.5500
Unit Testing is not a new concept. It’s been there since the early days of programming. Usually, developers and sometimes white box testers write Unit tests to improve code quality by verifying every unit of the code used to implement functional requirements (aka test driven development TDD or test-first development). Most of us might know the classic definition – “Unit Testing is the method of verifying the smallest piece of testable code against its purpose.” If the purpose or requirement fails then the unit test has failed. In simple words, it means – writing a piece of code (unit test) to verify the code (unit) written for implementing requirements.

[S2] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/best-online-software-testing-course/ | section=QA Software Testing Training Course – Best Online QA Training | score=0.4644
By Vijay Full Bio > Hello friends, as you all know, we always strive to give back the knowledge that we gain from our experiences to the testing fraternity. To extend the same philosophy, we are coming up with a more structured curriculum in the form of a Software testing course. It is going to include everything there is to know for you to become a perfect Software Tester . This software testing QA training course is designed by working professionals in a way that over 40+ hours it will progress from introducing you to the basics of software testing to advanced topics like Software configuration management, creating a test plan, test estimations, etc. along with an introduction and familiarity with Automation testing and tools like QTP and QC

[S3] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/how-to-set-defect-priority-and-severity-with-defect-triage-process/ | section=What Is Defect Severity And Priority | score=0.4624
In the English definition, priority is used to compare two things or conditions, where one needs to be given more importance than the other(s) and needs to be tackled/resolved first before moving on to the next one(s). Therefore, in the context of defects, the priority of a defect would indicate the urgency with which it would need to be fixed. Severity, by the English definition, is used to describe the gravity of an undesirable occurrence. Hence, with bugs, the severity of a bug would indicate the effect it has on the system in terms of its impact. Who defines these? QA classifies the defect under severity based on the complexity and criticality of the defects

[S4] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/state-transition-testing-technique-for-testing-complex-applications/ | section=State Transition Testing Technique with Example | score=0.4459

details.question: What is the difference between smoke testing and sanity testing?
details.reference_answer: Smoke testing is a broad and shallow set of tests performed on a new build to verify that critical functionality works and the build is stable enough for further testing. Sanity testing is a narrow and focused set of tests performed after minor changes or bug fixes to verify that specific functionality works as expected.

. If you want to learn about the real capabilities and limitations of your software, you have to navigate off the beaten path. If you meander without direction or purpose, you’ll spend much time wandering for precious little insight: you’ll be lost. This is a book about how to explore well. [S2] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/smoke-testing-and-sanity-testing-difference/ | section=Smoke Test VsBuild Acceptance Testing | score=0.4875
Smoke Testing is directly related to Build Acceptance Testing (BAT). In BAT, we do the same testing – to verify if the build has not failed and if the system is working fine or not. Sometimes, it happens that when a build is created, some issues get introduced and when it is delivered, the build doesn’t work for the QA. I would say that BAT is a part of a smoke check because if the system is failing, then how can you as a QA accept the build for testing? Not just the functionalities, the system itself has to work before the QA’s proceed with In-Depth Testing.

[S3] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/smoke-testing-and-sanity-testing-difference/ | section=Smoke Test Cycle | score=0.4635
The following flowchart explains the Smoke Testing Cycle. Once a build is deployed to QA, the basic cycle followed is that if the smoke test passes, the build is accepted by the QA team for further testing but if it fails, then the build is rejected until the reported issues are fixed. Test Cycle

[S4] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/unit-testing/ | section=What is Unit Testing? | score=0.4606
Unit Testing is not a new concept. It’s been there since the early days of programming. Usually, developers and sometimes white box testers write Unit tests to improve code quality by verifying every unit of the code used to implement functional requirements (aka test driven development TDD or test-first development). Most of us might know the classic definition – “Unit Testing is the method of verifying the smallest piece of testable code against its purpose.” If the purpose or requirement fails then the unit test has failed. In simple words, it means – writing a piece of code (unit test) to verify the code (unit) written for implementing requirements.

[S5] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/smoke-testing-and-sanity-testing-difference/ | section=Sanity Testing | score=0.4516
Sanity Testing is done when as a QA we do not have sufficient time to run all the test cases, be it Functional Testing , UI, OS or Browser Testing. Hence, we can define, “Sanity Testing as a test execution which is done to touch each implementation and its impact but not thoroughly or in-depth, it may include functional, UI, version, etc. testing depending on the implementation and its impact.” Don’t we all fall into a situation where we have to sign off in a day or two but the build for testing is still not released? Ah yes, I bet you must have also faced this situation at least once in your Software Testing experience. Well, I faced it a lot because my project(s) were mostly agile and at times we were asked to deliver it the same day
details.generated_answer:
Smoke testing and sanity testing are both important testing methodologies in software quality assurance, but they serve different purposes and are conducted under different circumstances.

**Smoke Testing** is primarily focused on verifying whether the basic functionalities of a build are working correctly. It is often referred to as Build Acceptance Testing (BAT), where the goal is to ensure that the build has not failed and that the system is functioning properly before proceeding to more in-depth testing. If the smoke test passes, the build is accepted for further testing; if it fails, the build is rejected until the issues are resolved [S2][S3].

**Sanity Testing**, on the other hand, is performed when there is insufficient time to execute all test cases. It involves a quick check to ensure that the specific functionalities are working as expected after changes have been made, but it does not cover all aspects in depth. Sanity testing may include various types of testing, such as functional and UI testing, depending on the implementation and its impact [S5].

In summary, smoke testing is a broader check of the overall functionality of a build, while sanity testing is a more focused assessment of specific functionalities when time is constrained.

details.question: What is non-functional testing?
details.reference_answer: Non-functional testing evaluates quality attributes of a system rather than specific functional behavior. It checks aspects such as performance, load handling, stress capacity, security, usability, compatibility, reliability, and installation behavior.

The application could also be impacted due to different versions, resolutions, internet speed configurations, etc. Hence it’s important to test the application in all possible manners to reduce failures and overcome the embarrassment of bug leakage. As a Non- functional test, Compatibility testing is to endorse that the application runs properly in different browsers, versions, OS, and networks successfully. Compatibility tests should always be performed in a real environment instead of a virtual environment
details.generated_answer:
Non-functional testing is a type of software testing that focuses on the non-functional aspects of an application, such as performance, usability, reliability, and compatibility. Its primary goal is to ensure that the software meets certain criteria that are not related to specific functionalities but are crucial for user satisfaction and overall system performance.

One specific area of non-functional testing is compatibility testing, which determines whether a software application can run effectively across different browsers, databases, hardware, operating systems, mobile devices, and networks. This type of testing is essential to reduce failures and prevent issues related to bug leakage, ensuring that the application operates correctly in various environments and configurations [S5]. 

Additionally, proper planning of the testing strategy is vital for non-functional testing to ensure comprehensive coverage of these aspects. Various tools are available to assist in performing non-functional tests, and it is an important part of the overall testing process that should not be overlooked [S2].

details.question: What is regression testing and when should it be performed?
details.reference_answer: Regression testing verifies that recent code changes, bug fixes, configuration updates, or new features have not broken existing functionality. It should be performed whenever software is modified, before releases, after bug fixes, and when integrations or environments change.

Regression Testing is usually performed after verification of changes or new functionality. But this is not always the case. For releases that are taking months to complete, regression tests must be incorporated into the daily test cycle. For weekly releases, regression tests can be performed when Functional Testing is over for the changes. Regression checking is a variation of retest (which is simply to repeat a test). When retesting, the reason can be anything. Say, you were testing a particular feature and it was the end of the day- you could not finish testing and had to stop the process without deciding if the test passed/failed. The next day when you come back, you perform the test once more – that means you are repeating a test you performed before

details.generated_answer:
Regression testing is a type of software testing that involves re-running previously conducted tests to ensure that recent changes or new functionalities have not adversely affected existing features. This process includes comparing current test results with those from previous executions to identify any discrepancies or defects introduced by the changes made in the software [S1][S4].

Regression testing should be performed after verification of changes or new functionality. For projects with lengthy release cycles, it is advisable to incorporate regression tests into the daily testing routine. In cases of weekly releases, regression tests can be conducted after the completion of functional testing for the changes made. Best practices suggest conducting regression tests after sanity or smoke testing and at the end of functional testing, especially for shorter release cycles [S1][S4].

details.question: What is requirements traceability matrix and why is it useful?
details.reference_answer: A Requirements Traceability Matrix, or RTM, maps requirements to test cases. It helps ensure test coverage, identify missing tests or uncovered requirements, support change impact analysis, and provide visibility into requirement validation status.

details.generated_answer:
A Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) is a tool used to ensure that all requirements defined for a system are tested throughout the development process. It maps test cases to their corresponding requirements, facilitating both forward and backward traceability. Forward traceability ensures that the project progresses according to the desired direction by confirming that every requirement is tested thoroughly. Backward traceability verifies that the current product being developed aligns with the specified requirements, preventing the addition of unspecified functionalities that could affect the project scope. A well-structured RTM provides bi-directional traceability, linking test cases back to requirements and vice versa, which is crucial for comprehensive testing and validation of the software product [S2].

The usefulness of an RTM lies in its ability to provide clear visibility into the testing process, ensuring that all requirements are covered by test cases. This helps in achieving maximum test coverage, which is essential for minimizing defects in the final product. Additionally, an RTM can assist in assessing the impact of any changes to business or functional requirements on the QA team's work, guiding them in revisiting or reworking test cases as necessary [S3][S5].

details.question: What is test coverage?
details.reference_answer: Test coverage measures how much of the application, requirements, code, or functionality is exercised by test cases. It helps identify gaps in testing and provides insight into whether important areas of the system have been validated.

[S1] | source=a_friendly_introduction_to_software_testing_9781523477371_compress.pdf | score=0.5500
14.9. CODE COVERAGE 127 From a code coverage perspective, we have 100% code coverage and 100% method coverage—the only method in the class is called from a test, and every statement in the method is executed. However, callingmoo() with amooLevel parameter of 0 will cause aDivideByZeroException to be thrown. This defect will not be discovered by the test case, despite every statement being executed. Although not foolproof, ensuring that you’ve checked every equivalence class will help to ameliorate situations like this. In fact, code coverage metrics can be even more misleading than this example. As long as a statement is executed by the test, that code is considered “covered”. Nothing verifies that the unit tests actually test anything, though

14.9. CODE COVERAGE 125 14.9 Code Coverage Code coverage tells you how much of the codebase is actually executed when running the test suite code. Since defining exactly what is meant by “how much of the codebase” can be complex, there are numerous kinds of code coverage. The simplest form of code coverage is method coverage; this measures what percentage of methods have any tests that call into them. For example, imagine a class Turtle with two methods,crawl() and eat(): public class Turtle { public void crawl() { ... } public void eat() { ... } } If we have a test callingcrawl(), but none callingeat(), then we have 50% code coverage (one of two methods was tested)

[S5] | source=https://www.softwaretestinghelp.com/test-coverage/ | section=What is Test Coverage in Software Testing? How to Improve It | score=0.4851
. Of them all, coverage is considered especially valuable. details.generated_answer:
Test coverage refers to the extent to which the requirements of customers are verified during the testing phase. It determines whether the test cases are adequately written and executed to ensure that the software application is tested comprehensively, aiming for minimal or no defects reported [S3]. 

To achieve maximum test coverage, it is essential to establish good requirement traceability. This involves mapping all internal defects to the designed test cases and linking customer-reported defects to individual test cases for future regression testing [S3]. 

Overall, test coverage is a critical metric in software testing that helps ensure that all aspects of the application are thoroughly evaluated.

details.question: How should test data be designed before executing test cases?
details.reference_answer: Test data should be prepared before execution to cover valid inputs, invalid inputs, boundary values, edge cases, and realistic business scenarios. Good test data design improves repeatability, avoids execution delays, and helps validate both normal and exceptional system behavior.

Test data should be designed by identifying and creating the necessary data based on the specific requirements of each project. This process typically involves several strategies, including:

- **Data from the Production Environment**: Utilizing real data from the existing production environment can help ensure that the test cases are relevant and realistic.
- **Retrieving SQL Queries**: Extracting data from the client's existing databases through SQL queries can provide the necessary test data.
- **Automated Data Generation Tools**: These tools can assist in generating the required data automatically, which can save time and effort.

It is essential for testers to back up their testing with complete data, considering the elements relevant to the project requirements [S4].
